Facts.text: Roman general Orestes forces western Roman Emperor Julius Nepos to flee his capital city, Ravenna, now seen as the end of the Roman Empire in the West
German ruler Flavius Odoacer captures Pavia
King of the Ostrogoths Theodoric the Great defeats Germanic King of Italy Odoacer at the Battle of Isonzo, forcing his way into Italy
Third Crusade: Crusaders begin the Siege of Acre under Guy of Lusignan
Belgrade captured by troops of Turkish Sultan Suleyman the Magnificent

Battle of Wofla, Turkish-Portuguese War: The Portuguese are defeated and forced to flee by the forces of Imam Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi in modern-day Ofla, Ethiopia. Portuguese commander Christovão da Gama is captured and later executed.
Henry Casimir I is appointed viceroy of Drenthe
WIC-colonel Hans Koin conquers Fort Elmina, West Africa
Director of the colony of New Netherland, Peter Stuyvesant, bars Jews from military service
Pharmacists John Lea and William Perrins manufacture Worcestershire sauce
Nine Jewish prisoners are released from Damascus jails
Scientific American magazine publishes its first issue
Venice under Daniele Manin surrenders to Austrians under Radetsky, having been under siege since July 20 after proclaiming independence
A geomagnetic storm causes the Aurora Borealis to shine so brightly that it is seen clearly over parts of the USA, Europe, and even as far afield as Japan
Battle of Hatteras Inlet Batteries: Union Army and Navy in their first combined operation take Fort Clark in North Carolina
Battle of Groveton, Virginia (Manassas Plains)
Battle of Thoroughfare Gap, Virginia
Belle Boyd is released from Old Capitol Prison in Washington, D.C.
The United States formally takes control of Midway Atoll, years after it was sighted and claimed by Captain N.C. Brooks
MLB pitcher Mickey Welch sets a record for most consecutive batters struck out to begin a game, striking out the first nine men he faces
Caleb Bradham renames his carbonated soft drink "Pepsi-Cola"
United Parcel Service is founded by James E. Casey in Seattle, Washington
Nicholas I of Montenegro again proclaims himself king (first assumed power 1860) after his reign interrupted by Turkish rule
World War I: Battle of Helgoland Bight (North Sea); British fleet decisively defeats Germans; nearly 800 die, and over 200 are wounded
Germany declares war on Romania
Italy declares war on Germany
Ten suffragists are arrested while picketing the White House
MLB Cleveland Indians outfielderTris Speakeris suspended for the season due to an assault on umpire Tom Connolly

American swimmer Norman Ross wins his second of three gold medals at the Antwerp Olympics, beating teammate Ludwig Langer in the men's 400 m freestyle, and also wins the 1500 m and 4 x 200 m freestyle relay
Babe Ruthstarts a streak of hitting an extra-base hit in nine consecutive games
Second Pan-African Congress meets in London, Brussels, and Paris
Albert von Tilzer and Neville Fleeson's musical "The Gingham Girl" premieres in New York City
WEAF in New York City airs the first radio commercial for Queensboro Realty, costing $100 for 10 minutes
Georgian opposition stages the August Uprising against the Soviet Union
Meteorite falls on Ellemeet, Schouwen, Devil's Island
Emil Levsen pitches a complete doubleheader victory for the Indians against the Red Sox
English cricket all-rounder Frank Woolley scores 176 for Kent against Middlesex at Lord's in his 100th first-class hundred
Toyota Motors becomes an independent company
Mauthausen concentration camp opens in Austria
Northwestern University awards honorary degree to dummy Charlie McCarthy
On Connie Mack Day at Philadelphia's Shibe Park, the A's win a doubleheader

Journalist Clare Hollingworth observes "large numbers of troops, literally hundreds of tanks, armored cars, and field guns" Germany has aligned along the Polish border. Three days later, Hitler invades Poland and WWII begins.
Netherlands mobilizes
Sammy Fainand Jack Yellen's musical "George White's Scandals" premieres in New York City
French colonies Cameroon and Congo-Brazzaville support Gen. de Gaulle
8th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Chicago Bears 37, All-Stars 13 (98,203)
Last meeting of resistance fighter Comte d'Estienne d'Orves
9th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Chicago Bears 21, All-Stars 0 (101,100 attendees)
Swedish runner Gunder Hägg sets a world record of 8:01.2 in the 3000 m
Transport #25 departs with French Jews to Nazi Germany
Denmark declares a general strike against Nazi occupiers
Last German troops in Marseille surrender, and Toulon is cleared
US air raid on Ambon Island, Dutch East Indies
Earle and Roy Mack purchase 54% of the Philadelphia A's from Connie Mack Jr.
Braves sell pitcher Johnny Sain to the Yankees for $50,000
Pirates snap NY Giants' 16-game winning streak
Germany and Israel reach an agreement on reparations payments
Yakov Malik succeeds Valerian Zorin as Soviet Foreign Minister
Rams beat Giants 23-17 in the first NFL preseason sudden-death football game
17th Venice Film Festival opens: no Golden Lion awarded
Strom Thurmond, a Democratic senator from South Carolina, begins the longest filibuster of 24 hours and 18 minutes against the Civil Rights Act of 1957
American baseball player Nellie Fox sets a record for consecutive games without striking out (98)
White Sox's Ted Kluszewski's three-run home run is disallowed as the umpire calls time
55.9 cm of rainfall recorded in Hackberry, Louisiana (state record)
Dr. Geza de Kaplany tortures his wife with acid to punish her for supposed infidelity
Evergreen Point Floating Bridge connecting Seattle and Bellevue opens

Race riot in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, sparked by police reaction to a domestic dispute, fuels a traffic tie-up; riots and looting continue for 3 days, with 2 killed, hundreds injured, and $4 million in damage in the city's North Philadelphia neighborhood
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
US weather satellite Nimbus 1 is launched
First Subway sandwich shop opens in Bridgeport, Connecticut
Asif Iqbal and Intikhab Alam make a 190-run stand for the 9th wicket against England
MLB Boston Red Sox sign first free-agent outfielder Ken Harrelson for a $75,000 bonus
WCBS-AM (880) radio in NYC begins its all-news format; the AM station is knocked off the air just beforehand, moving the debut to the FM sister station (101.1)
Police and anti-war demonstrators clash at Chicago's Democratic National Convention
"I'll Be There" single by The Jackson 5 is released (Billboard Song of the Year 1970)
Phillies' Larry Bowa steals home for the second time in 1970
The US dollar is allowed to float against the Japanese yen for the first time
Australian teenage swimmerShane Gouldsets a world record of 2:23.07 to beat German Kornelia Ender in the 200 m individual medley at the Munich Olympics; her first of three gold medals at the Games
North Korean shooter Ri Ho-jun wins the 50 m rifle prone event at the Munich Olympics; his nation's first Olympic gold medal
USSR performs a nuclear test in Novaya Zemlya, USSR
"Smoke on the Water" single by British rock band Deep Purple goes gold
A 6.8-magnitude quake centered in Oaxaca State, Mexico, kills 527
Bobby "Boris" Pickett's song "Monster Mash" goes gold
France conducts a nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
India and Pakistan sign a prisoner of war agreement
Norrmalmstorg robbery and its six-day siege end in Stockholm with a successful police tear-gas operation, the first Swedish crime broadcast live on TV and the origin of the term Stockholm Syndrome
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
Soyuz 15 returns to Earth
Toronto Metros-Croatia beat the Minnesota Kicks to the win the 1976 North American Soccer League championship
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
MLB California Angels pitcherNolan Ryanstrikes out 300 batters for the fifth consecutive year
New York Cosmos defeat Seattle Sounders 2-1 at Civic Stadium in Portland, Oregon, winning their second North American Soccer League championship
NY YankeeRon Guidryfaces just 28 batters in 1-0 win over Texas Rangers
Donald Vesco rides a 21-foot-long Kawasaki motorcycle at 318.598 mph (512.73 km/h)
Ja'afar Sharif-Emami is appointed premier of Iran
IRA bomb explodes in Brussels' Grand Place
Train crash in Nijmegen, Netherlands, kills 7
First use of a Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) machine to scan the human body at Aberdeen Royal Infirmary, Scotland
National Centers for Disease Control announce a high incidence of Pneumocystis and Kaposi's sarcoma in gay men
First Gay Games are held in San Francisco
The USSR performs an underground nuclear test
Greg Luzinski becomes the first player to hit three home runs onto the roof at Comiskey Park, Chicago
Joseph Kreckman sets a record of 2,215 clay pigeons shot in an hour
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
Bolivian President Víctor Paz Estenssoro calls a state of siege
US Navy officer Jerry A. Whitworth is sentenced to 365 years for espionage
Mike Schmidtsurpasses Ted Williams and Willie McCovey with 522 home runs
40th Emmy Awards: thirtysomething, The Wonder Years, and Richard Kiley win
70 people are killed in a crash of three Italian Air Force fighters at an air show in Ramstein, Germany
Eric Meeks wins the 88th US Golf Amateur Championship
First regular-season matchup of defending Cy Young Award winners
Frank Viola and the NY Mets outduelOrel Hershiserand the LA Dodgers, winning 1-0
Chicago Cubs'Ryne Sandbergis the first second baseman to hit 30 home runs in consecutive seasons
F5 strength tornado hits Plainfield, Illinois, killing 29 and injuring 353
Lexington Avenue IRT subway train derails at Union Square, killing 5
Red Tom Browning vs. Expos'Dennis Martínez, both perfect game pitchers
Brewers beat Blue Jays 22-2 with an AL record of 31 hits in 9 innings
Test cricket debut of Muttiah Muralitharan against Australia in Colombo
Dam breaks in Qinghai, West China, killing 223 people
Deputy Prime Minister of Singapore Ong Teng Cheong is elected president
Jakovlev-42 crashes in Tajikistan, killing 76
Long Beach, California, defeats Panama for the Little League World Championship
First Japanese Gay Pride Parade
Kuwaiti Oil Minister Abdul Mohsen al-Medej announces that his country will increase its oil production capacity to as much as 3.5 million barrels per day by 2005
Last day of Test Cricket for Richie Richardson
Northants 7-781 declared defeat Nottinghamshire 527 and 157
Belgian amusement park riders are stuck upside down for 90 minutes
Officially 98 and unofficially up to 400 villagers are massacred in Rais by the Armed Islamic Group in one of Algeria's bloodiest massacres
Pakistan's National Assembly passes a constitutional amendment to make the "Qur'an and Sunnah" the "supreme law," but the bill is defeated in the Senate
Philip Glass's Symphony No. 5 "Requiem, Bardo, Nirmanakaya" for chorus and orchestra premieres at the Salzburg Festival, led by Dennis Russell Davies in Salzburg, Austria
Electricity blackout cuts off power to around 500,000 people living in southeast England and brings 60% of London's Underground rail network to a halt
A huge upset at the Athens Olympic Stadium in the men's 4×100 m relay; Great Britain (38.07) edges the United States (38.08) to win the gold medal
British athlete Kelly Holmes wraps up the 800/1500 m double at the Athens Olympics, winning the 1500 m in 3:57.90
Carlos Tevez scores the winner as Argentina beats Paraguay 1-0, maintaining an undefeated record to win the men's football gold medal at the Athens Olympics
Led by San Antonio Spurs shooting guard Manu Ginóbili, Argentina beats Italy 84-69 for the Olympic basketball gold medal in Athens; star-studded US team wins bronze
Moroccan super star athlete Hicham El Guerrouj wraps up the 1500/5000 m double at the Athens Olympics winning the 5000 m gold in 13:14.39
The diving competition at the Athens Olympics concludes with the Chinese contingent dominant, winning 6 of the 8 gold medals
The US women's basketball team goes through the Athens Olympics undefeated to win its fifth Olympic gold medal, beating Australia 74-63 in the final
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, Reserve Vineyards & GC: Loren Roberts wins the first of four Champions Tour majors with a bogey on the second playoff hole against Dana Quigley
51 people are killed in a series of bombings across Iraq
China and Russia walk out of a UN Security Council meeting after the US pushes for immediate action against Syria's alleged use of chemical weapons
English actor Ed Skrein pulls out of the role in the movie "Hellboy" after whitewashing criticism
Investigation into German nurse Niels Högel, serving a life sentence for killing two patients, concludes he probably killed 86 more
Kenya enacts the world's toughest ban on plastic bags with a possible US$38,000 fine and four years in jail
Matt Vogel debuts as the voice of Kermit the Frog in the "Muppet Thought of the Week" video
North Korea launches a missile that flies over Japan, and the country's J-Alert warning system warns people to take cover
Chinese ride-sharing firm Didi Chuxing issues a public apology after a second Hitch passenger is killed within three months
Puerto Rico raises the official death toll of the 2017 Hurricane Maria from 64 to 2,975

Discovery of a 3.8-million-year-old skull of the early human ancestor Australopithecus anamensis, found by Yohannes Haile-Selassie at Miro Dora, Ethiopia, upends previous evolutionary theory published in the journal "Nature"
US Senator Kirsten Gillibrand ends her campaign for president
Japanese tech company SkyDrive says it completed the first manned test flight of a flying car

MLB Los Angeles Angels pitcher and designated hitterShohei Ohtanibecomes the first player in team history to reach 20 stolen bases and hit 40 home runs in a season during a 10-2 win over the San Diego Padres
Pakistan appeals for international aid as the death toll from monsoon rains and floods rises to over 1,000 people
Barbie becomes Warner Bros' highest-grossing global release, overtaking Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows Part 2, earning $1.34 billion
2024 Summer Paralympics, the 17th Paralympic Games, open in Paris, France
South Korean K-pop singer Taeil leaves his band Neo Culture Technology (NCT) after charges in a “criminal case related to a sexual crime”
The US completes its largest dam removal project on the Klamath River at the California-Oregon border, advocated for by tribal nations to restore the salmon population
Amtrak launches its NextGen Acela trainsets for servicing the US Northeast Corridor; premium high-speed trains between Washington, D.C. and Boston are capable of speeds up to 160 MPH
Houthi self-proclaimed Prime Minister Ahmed al-Rahawi is killed in an Israeli airstrike in Sanaa, Yemen
Immersive AI-enhanced version of 1939 film "The Wizard of Oz" opens at Sphere in Las Vegas, Nevada
MLB Philadelphia Phillies slugger Kyle Schwarber hits 4 home runs, with personal and franchise record 9 RBI's in 19-4 win over visiting Atlanta Braves at Citizens Bank Park
King Johnof England grants small town of Liverpool a charter (right to elect a mayor and aldermen)
English explorerHenry Hudsonis the first European to sail into Delaware Bay, naming it South Bay
Ferdinand IIis elected Holy Roman Emperor and rules until 1637
Second Bishop's War: KingCharles I's English army loses to a Scottish Covenanter force in the Battle of Newburn
William Herscheldiscovers Saturn's moon Enceladus
Richard Wagner's opera "Lohengrin" premieres in Weimar, Germany
QueenWilhelmina of the Netherlandsopens the Peace Palace in The Hague, Netherlands, an international law administrative building housing the Permanent Court of Arbitration
World War I: British GeneralJohn Frenchorders the civilian evacuation of Amiens, France, as German forces push in from Belgium
World War I: Third day of the Battle ofTannenbergnear Allenstein, East Prussia (present-day Poland) with violent German vs. Russian battles
Ethelda Bleibtreyleads an American medal sweep in the Antwerp Olympics women's 300 m freestyle with a world record swim of 4:34.0
Gen.Bernard Montgomery("Monty") becomes commander of the 3rd "Iron" Infantry Division
Benito Mussoliniis transferred from La Maddalena, Sardinia, to Gran Sasso
Film noir "The Killers," based on a story byErnest Hemingway, directed by Robert Siodmak and starringBurt LancasterandAva Gardner, premieres
Riot preventsPaul Robesonfrom singing near Peekskill, New York
Chicago Black teenagerEmmett Tillis kidnapped, beaten, and shot dead by white men in Money, Mississippi; his killers are eventually acquitted, but the case helps ignite the US civil rights movement.
Tony Sheridanand The Beat Brothers (also known as The Beatles) record "Ya Ya" (Parts 1 & 2) in Hamburg, Germany
Bob Dylanis booed for playing electric guitar during a concert at Forest Hills Tennis Stadium in Queens, New York City

Northern Irish MPGerry Fitttables a House of Commons motion criticizing the Royal Ulster Constabulary at Dungannon, stating that "citizens of Northern Ireland should be allowed the same rights of peaceful demonstration as those in other parts of the United Kingdom"
American 4 x 100 m freestyle relay team of David Edgar, John Murphy, John Heidenreich, andMark Spitzswim a world record 3:26.42 to beat the Soviet Union for the gold medal at the Munich Olympics
Soviet gymnastOlga Korbutbecomes a media darling at the Munich Olympics, winning gold in the team all-around, followed by two individual golds and a silver
Tamla/Motown Records releases "Let's Get It On," the 13th studio album byMarvin Gaye
Britain'sSebastian Coesets a one-mile world record of 3:47.33
Israeli Prime MinisterMenachem Beginannounces his resignation
American rock singerTina Turnerreceives a star on Hollywood's Walk of Fame in Hollywood, California
The Venetian Macao, the second-largest casino in the world, owned byLas Vegas Sands, opens in Macao
Noel Gallagher, the guitarist of the British pop-rock bandOasis, quits the band, citing an inability to be with his brother Liam
Mitt Romneyis officially nominated as the United States Republican Party's candidate
Aretha Franklinlies in state at the Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History in Detroit in a 24-karat gold coffin
British Prime MinisterBoris Johnsonprorogues Parliament, suspending it for five weeks to limit opposition to a no-deal Brexit, prompting a furious backlash
Climate change activistGreta Thunbergarrives in New York after sailing across the Atlantic in an emissions-free voyage
New Italian coalition government formed by the anti-establishment Five Star Movement and the center-left Democratic Party oustsMatteo Salvini
A 1952Mickey Mantlebaseball card sells for $12.6 million at auction, becoming the world's most expensive piece of sports memorabilia
Orestes, father of Emperor Romulus Augustulus is captured and executed by Odoacer and his followers
The first American-built locomotive, "Tom Thumb," races a horse-drawn car from the Stockton and Stokes stagecoach company from Baltimore to Ellicott Mills; due to mechanical problems, the horse wins!
Britain's Slavery Abolition Act gains royal assent
First known photograph of a tornado is taken near Howard, South Dakota
Martin Luther King Jr.delivers his "I Have a Dream" speech during the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom civil rights march at the Lincoln Memorial in Washington, D.C.
John Hinckley Jr. pleads not guilty to all charges in the attempted assassination of US PresidentRonald Reagan
Rhazes [Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn Zakariyyāʾ al-Rāzī], Persian physician, philosopher, and writer, born in Ray, Persia (d. 925 or 935)
Go-Reizei, 70th Emperor of Japan (1045-68), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1068)
Kanō Motonobu, Japanese painter and calligrapher (Kanō school of painting), born in Kyoto, Japan (d. 1559)
Taichang Emperor [Zhu Changluo], 14th Emperor of the Ming dynasty of China, born in Beijing (d. 1620)
Marcus Zuerius van Boxhorn, Dutch scholar, born in Bergen op Zoom, Netherlands (d. 1653)
Louise of Mecklenburg-Güstrow, queen of Denmark and Norway, born in Güstrow, Germany (d. 1721)
Elisabeth Christine of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel, Holy Roman Empire Empress, born in Braunschweig, Germany (d. 1750)
Charlotte Christine of Brunswick-Lüneburg, Wife of Tsarevich Alexei Petrovich of Russia, born in Wolfenbüttel, Germany (d. 1715)
Carolomannus Pachschmidt, Austrian composer, born in Eisenstadt, Austria (d. 1734)
Louis-Auguste d'Affry, French general and ambassador to Netherland, born in Versailles, France (d. 1793)

Peter Winter, German composer, born in Mannhein, Electorate of the Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1825)
Sophie Gail, French singer and composer, born in Paris (d. 1819)
Stéphanie de Beauharnais, Grand Duchess of Baden, born in Versailles, France (d. 1860)
Johannes Zwijsen, Dutch archbishop (Utrecht Netherlands), born in Kerkdriel, Netherlands (d. 1877)
Joannes Paredis, Belgian Bishop of Roermond, born in Bree, Belgium (d. 1886)
Gershom Jaques Van Brunt, U.S. Army (Union Navy), born in Monmouth County, New Jersey (d. 1863)

Carel van Nispen of Sevenaer, Dutch Catholic politician, born in Zevenaar, Netherlands (d. 1884)

Grand Duchess Catherine Mikhailovna of Russia, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1894)

Albert Dietrich, German composer and conductor, born in Meissen, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1908)
Fredrick Vilhelm Ludvig Norman, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1885)
Lucy Ware Webb Hayes, 1st lady of the United States (1877-81), born in Chillicothe, Ohio (d. 1889)

Alexander Cameron Sim, Scottish merchant, born in Aberlour, Scotland (d. 1900)
Ira D. Sankey, American evangelist, composer and singer (Gospel hymns), born in Edinburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1908)
Louis Le Prince, French artist and original inventor of the motion picture camera sometimes called "the father of cinematography", born in Metz, France (d. 1890)
Willem Linnig The Younger, Flemish painter and graphic artist, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1890)
Vladimir Shukhov, Russian engineer and inventor, born in Grayvoron, Russia (d. 1939)
Umberto Giordano, Italian composer, chiefly of opera (Andrea Chénier; Fedora; La cena delle beffe), born in Foggia, Apulia, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1948)
Alfred Baldwin Sloan, American stage composer ("Heaven Will Protect the Working Girl"), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1925)
Helen Haye [Hay], British stage and silent and sound screen actress (Honour in Pawn; Girl in the Taxi; Spy in Black), born in Assam, British India (d. 1957)

Daniel Zamudio, Colombian organist, composer and folklorist, born in Bogotá, Colombia (d. 1952)

Pietro Deiro, Italian-American accordionist (1st president of the American Accordionists Association), and music publisher (Accordion Music Publishing Company (AMPCO)), born in Salto Canavese, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1954)
Ivor Gurney, English composer (Severn Meadows; Seven Sappho Songs) and poet (War's Embers), born in Gloucester, England (d. 1937)

Rudolph Wagner-Régeny, composer, born in Transylvania, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1969)
Secondo Campini, Italian jet engine pioneer, born in Bologna, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1980)
Cyril Walters, Welsh cricket batsman (11 Tests England, 1 x 100, HS 102; Glamorgan CCC, Worcestershire CCC), born in Bedlinog, Wales (d. 1992)
Sam Levene, American Broadway actor (Demon, Gung Ho, Boomerang), born in Russia (d. 1980)
John Betjeman, poet (Mount Zion) and English Poet Laureate (1972-1984), born in London (d. 1984)
Klaas Schenk, Dutch speed skating coach (1952, 56, 60 Winter Olympics; father of triple gold medallist Ard), born in Wieringerwaard, Hollands Kroon, Netherlands (d. 1983)
Leslie Dwyer, English actor (Hi-de-Hi!), born in London, England (d. 1986)
Phil Edwards, Canadian-Guyanese athlete (5 x Olympic bronze 1928, 32, 36), born in Georgetown, Guyana (d. 1971)
Orestes López, Cuban band leader and composer (co-inventor of 'mambo' with his brother, Cachao), born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1991)
Roger Tory Peterson, American ornithologist and writer (How to Know Birds), born in Jamestown, New York (d. 1996)
Lamberto Maggiorani, Italian factory worker turned actor (Antonio in "Bicycle Thieves"), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1983)
Tjalling Koopmans, Dutch-American economist (Nobel 1975), born in 's-Graveland, Netherlands (d. 1985)
Jose Antonio Girón, Spanish politician (Minister of Labour, 1941-57), born in Herrera de Pisuerga, Spain (d. 1995)
Joseph Luns, Dutch Foreign Minister and Secretary General (NATO), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 2002)
Ruurd Faber, Dutch politcian and Mayor of Aalten (1971-75), born in Veenhuizen, Netherlands (d. 1992)
John Terence Reese, British bridge master, born in Epsom, Surrey, England (d. 1996)
Lindsay Hassett, Australian cricket batsman and captain (43 Tests; 3,073 runs	@ 46.56; 10 x 100s; Victoria) and broadcaster (ABC 1956-81), born in Geelong, Australia (d. 1993)
Richard Tucker [Rivn Ticker], American tenor (New York Metroploitan Opera, 1945-75), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1975)
Robertson Davies, Canadian novelist essayist and dramatist, born in Thamesville, Ontario (d. 1995)
(Abraham) "Glenn" Osser, American arranger and orchestra leader (Paul Whiteman's Goodyear Revue; Miss America Pageant), born in Munising, Michigan (d. 2014)
Max Robertson, British sports commentator (BBC Radio), born in Dacca, Bengal (d. 2009)
Simon Oakland [Weiss], American stage and screen character actor (Toma; Kolchak), born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 1983)
Tasha Tudor, American illustrator and children's books author (Pumpkin Moonshine, Corgiville Fair), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2008)
C. Wright Mills, American sociologist and writer (The Power Elite), born in Waco, Texas (d. 1962)
Hélène Baillargeon, Canadian-Quebecoise singer and folklorist, born in Saint-Martin, Quebec (d. 1997)
Jack [Holbrook] Vance, American sci-fi author (2 Hugo, Dirdir, Pnume), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2013)
Alejandro Agustín Lanusse, President of Argentina (1971-73), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1996)
Ernest Harold Martin [Markowitz], American Broadway producer (Feuer & Martin-Chorus Line), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1995)
Godfrey Hounsfield, English electrical engineer and inventor (CT scan, 1979 Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine), born in Newark, England (d. 2004)[1]
Frits Bernard, Dutch clinical psychologist and child exploitation activist, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 2006)
Fernando Fernán Gómez, Spanish actor, director, academic and playwright (All About My Mother), born in Lima, Peru (d. 2007)
Johan "Joop" Doderer, Dutch actor (Swiebertje; My Fair Lady), born in Velsen, Netherlands (d. 2005)
John Herbert Chapman, Canadian physicist, and space researcher, born in London, Ontario (d. 1979)
Nancy Kulp, American actress (The Beverly Hillbillies, The Aristocats, Shane), born in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1991)
(Margaret) "Peggy" Ryan, American dancer (The Merry Monahans; Here Come The Co-eds), and actress (Hawaii Five-0), born in Long Beach, California (d. 2004)

Janet Frame, New Zealand novelist (Intensive Care, Owls Do Cry), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 2004)
Tony MacGibbon, New Zealand cricket fast bowler (26 Tests, 70 wickets; Canterbury CA), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 2010)
Vilayat Khan, Indian classical sitar player and composer, born in Gouripur, Mymensingh, East Bengal (d. 2004)
Arkady Natanovich Strugatsky, Soviet-Russian sci-fi author (Tale of the Troika), born in Batumi, Georgia (d. 1991)
Billy Grammer, American country music singer and guitarist, born in Benton, Illinois (d. 2011)
Donald O'Connor, American dancer and actor (Singin' in the Rain), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2003)
John Gillett, British film critic and researcher, born in London (d. 1995)
Marvin Davis, CEO (Davis Oil Company), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 2004)
William J. Rutter, American biochemist and co-founder of the Chiron Corporation, born in Malad City, Idaho (d. 2025)[1]
F. William Free, American advertising executive (I'm Cheryl – Fly Me.), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2003)
